description: This figure presents bright-field (BF) and weak-beam BF TEM micrographs characterizing dislocation morphologies in the hot-rolled Fe-15.5Mn-3.95Al-5Ti-2.15B steel. Panels (a)-(a2) show delta-ferrite (BCC): (a) exhibits dense dislocation tangles within a grain distant from TiB2 particles, with a BCC <001> SADP inset; (a1) and (a2) reveal dislocation pile-ups and tangles at the delta-ferrite/TiB2 interface, with weak-beam imaging in (a2) at g = 101 (caption states) and a BCC <111> SADP. Panels (b)-(b2) show austenite (FCC): (b) displays sparse dislocations away from TiB2, with an FCC <011> SADP inset; (b1) and (b2) show pile-ups and sparser tangles at the austenite/TiB2 interface (marked by a blue line in b1), with weak-beam imaging in (b2) at g = 11\bar{1} (caption states). The comparison establishes that delta-ferrite harbors substantially higher dislocation density than austenite after hot rolling, and delta-ferrite/TiB2 interfaces accumulate denser dislocation networks than austenite/TiB2 interfaces. SADP evidence indicates a crystallographic orientation relationship at delta-ferrite/TiB2 interfaces consistent with coherent or semi-coherent boundaries, whereas austenite/TiB2 interfaces lack such a relationship. These findings confirm heterogeneous dislocation distribution and interface-dependent accumulation, supporting the role of grain-boundary-distributed TiB2 in the steel's initial deformation resistance.
